frontmatter.title: SEEK // Pursuer
frontmatter.summary: A glossy black humanoid pursuit entity that turns route reading into a panic-compression test.

assetCredits.title: Forgotten Memories of Exploring a Liminal Space.jpg
assetCredits.creator: Michael Savidge

entities.body:
Seek is preceded by clusters of watchful eyes and a sharp change in corridor pressure. Once materialized, the entity punishes hesitation more reliably than slow reflexes.

The safe response is simple and difficult: stop looting, read the next opening, crouch before obstacles, and never turn around to inspect the threat.

## Field Notes

- Warning eyes should be treated as a countdown.
- Blue-lit or visually emphasized doors are usually the safe route.
- Sound confirms pursuit state, but visual geometry decides survival.
- The most common failure is entering the chase with attention split between loot and navigation.

frontmatter.title: HOUND // Backrooms Hound
frontmatter.summary: A low-profile hostile entity associated with pursuit corridors, noise lures, and poor visibility.
frontmatter.route: Noise contact -> line-of-sight break -> exit drift

assetCredits.title: Corridor on the second level - NAL hospital 3.jpg
assetCredits.creator: W.carter

entities.body:
The Hound record exists as the first non-Roblox expansion slot in the MVP matrix. It gives the content model room to support creepypasta entities, fan maps, and game-specific variants without changing the schema.

## Field Notes

- Avoid sprinting unless the entity has confirmed line of sight.
- Take wide turns to force reacquisition.
- Treat dead-silent corridors as possible reset zones.

frontmatter.title: Guide: Level 0 Escape Route
frontmatter.summary: Beginner-safe survival guide for Backrooms Level 0: orientation, loop detection, resource discipline, and exit pattern recognition.

guides.body:
Level 0 is dangerous because it makes almost-correct choices feel identical. The correct survival posture is not bravery; it is boring consistency.

## First Minute

Stand still, choose one visual anchor, and listen for stable sound sources. Your first goal is preventing circular movement.

## Navigation Logic

If three landmarks repeat in the same order, stop and reverse for two room transitions. Then pick the first branch with different ceiling geometry.

## Resource Discipline

Walk by default. Save light for decision points, not straight corridors. Keep notes because Level 0 is visually simple enough to make memory unreliable.

## Exit Patterns

Valid exits usually create a sensory break: different floor sound, changed air pressure, altered light color, or a new mechanical rhythm.
